retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Additional Access Cavity Methods
retrieved_docs.content: RestoAC applies to restored teeth without active caries. In this method, entry to the pulp chamber is created by eliminating existing restorations either completely or partially, depending on the extent of structural compromise. The technique emphasizes preservation of any remaining intact tooth structure while still achieving adequate canal access. [71] [72]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional technique complications
retrieved_docs.content: Craniocervical perforation may develop when the convenience extension is left unfinished before the bur shaft enters the canal space. [63] [61] [62] Pulp debris left behind during preparation may also discolor the crown, especially when the access cavity lacks proper incisal extension and lies too close to the gingival margin. [63] [62] [40] Misjudgment of the tooth's lingual-axial or mesial-axial inclination can result in abrasion of the labial or distal wall during instrumentation. [63] [61] [62] Failure to complete the convenience extension can create a ledge at the apical-labial curve, where the instrument shaft rests on the shoulder or cavity edge instead of advancing smoothly into the canal. [63] [61] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: An additional cusp called the cusp of Carabelli is sometimes located on the mesio-palatal surface of permanent maxillary first molars and is rarely present on the other permanent molars. Although less common, an accessory cusp can protrude from the cingulum of the primary or permanent anterior teeth, referred to as the talon cusp. [26] Macrodontia and microdontia are rare shape anomalies of dentition, where teeth are larger or smaller than average. [27] [28] Taurodontism is an abnormality of tooth morphology that often occurs in multi-rooted teeth and is characterized by a short root and an enlarged body containing an equally enlarged pulp chamber. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional Methods for Anterior Access Cavity Creation
retrieved_docs.content: A fissure bur shapes the cavity outline incisally in a funnel or fan configuration. [54] [57] [58] Penetration into the pulp chamber proceeds with a surgical-length No. 2 or No. 4 round bur mounted on a slow-speed contra-angle handpiece. In cases of extensive pulpal recession, a No. 2 bur provides better control. Extension toward the incisal allows the bur shaft to operate nearly parallel to the long axis of the tooth. [38] [59] [57] Following penetration, the remaining tooth structure is removed with a round bur, including dentin beneath the lip of the chamber roof. [38] [59] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Dens invaginatus has various clinical presentations, which depend on the severity of the in-folding or the phase of morphodifferentiation where the invagination occurs. Several classifications exist to describe the extent of the in-folded tissue. The most utilized system is the Oehlers classification due to its ease of application. [18] In Type 1, the invagination ends as a blind sac and is limited to the coronal aspect of the tooth. Type 2 invagination extends beyond the CEJ and is retained within the main root canal of the tooth. Type 3 occurs when the invagination of dental tissue extends throughout the interior aspect of the root canal, sometimes even reaching the apical third of the tooth, potentially resulting in multiple apical foramina. [19]

retrieved_docs.content: Dentinogenesis imperfecta, especially DI type II, presents with greater pulpal tissue, which may complicate restorative interventions due to the higher frequency of pulpal exposure during restorative treatment or as a result of parafunctional habits.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – The mandibular second molars(tooth T, K) morphologically resemble the first mandibular molar of the permanent teeth. Its buccal surface is divided into the mesiobuccal, distobuccal, and distal cusps. The lingual cusps include the distolingual and the mesiolingual cusps. Numerous differences exist between the primary and permanent teeth, for example: Primary teeth are smaller Crowding is uncommon in primary teeth due to adequate space Primary teeth are whiter Pulp chambers of primary teeth are larger The enamel and dentin of primary teeth are thinner The area of contact of the primary molars are flat and broad The roots of the primary molar teeth are narrow and slender Pulp size relative to the crown is larger in primary teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Truss Access Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: The TrussAC method is generally applied in multiple-rooted teeth to maintain the dentinal bridge between 2 or more small cavities that have been prepared to access the canal orifices in each root. [7] [43] [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – Morphologically, the primary maxillary second molars (tooth A, J) resemble the permanent maxillary first molar. Rhomboidal in shape, the maxillary second molar has four cusps, two on both the buccal and lingual aspects. A fifth cusp can be present and is called the tubercle of Carabelli. Two well-defined cusps can be seen on the buccal view, and the lingual surface has three cusps: the mesiolingual, distolingual, and the cusp of Carabelli.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Conservative Access Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: In this method, a restricted amount of dentine can be removed from the chamber roof, allowing for the location of root canals without always requiring straight-line access. The access is extended so that the canals can be identified without completely exposing the pulp. The walls of an access cavity may be divergent or convergent. Only a partial part of the pulp chamber is removed. Regarding paracervical dentine removal, specifically, this design reduces the amount of dentine removed. [52] [66] [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional Methods for Anterior Access Cavity Creation
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, "adult" teeth exhibit ovoid pulp chambers partially filled with secondary dentin. To enable the bur shaft and instruments to function in the central axis, convenience extension must be advanced further initially when the radiograph shows advanced pulpal recession. The preparation funnels down toward the canal orifice, which becomes increasingly difficult to reach with a round bur as the pulp recedes, often necessitating a deeper initial extension. [56] [59] [57] Such an extension ensures that the bur shaft and instruments remain aligned with the central axis, even in cases with a reduced pulp space. [56] [59] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Classification -- Classification of Access Cavities for Anterior Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Access cavity designs in anterior teeth have evolved from conventional methods to minimally invasive techniques. [8] [9] The main classifications include: Traditional access cavity (TradAC) : TradAC involves the complete removal of the pulp chamber roof to provide direct, unobstructed entry to the root canal. Conservative access cavity (ConsAC) : ConsAC retains a portion of the pulp chamber roof and pericervical dentin while allowing adequate access for instrumentation. Ultraconservative access cavity (UltraAC) : UltraAC minimizes removal of the pulp chamber roof and surrounding dentin to preserve as much tooth structure as possible. Truss access cavity (TrussAC): This design creates 2 or more small, separate openings into individual canals while leaving truss-like sections of the pulp chamber roof intact.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Additional Access Cavity Methods
retrieved_docs.content: Other methods of cavity access include the caries-driven access cavity (CariesAC) and the restorative-driven access cavity (RestoAC). CariesAC involves removing only the carious tissue to gain access to the pulp chamber, while preserving all sound tooth structures. This approach limits unnecessary removal of dentin or enamel, prioritizing the conservation of natural anatomy during access. [70] [71] [72]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The maxillary first molars (tooth B, I) morphologically resemble both a molar and a premolar. Its occlusal surface consists of the mesiobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and distobuccal cusps. The largest cusp is the mesiolingual cusp, and it also represents the sharpest cusp. The distolingual cusp is small and rounded in morphology. The buccal surface is smooth, and the three roots are long and widely spread. Of note, the primary 1st molar has a less pronounced mesiobuccal cusp when compared to the permanent 1st molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The TrussAC is recommended when maintaining structural integrity is paramount, yet entry into multiple canals is required. This approach is most applicable in situations where canal orientations allow separate, small entry points and where restorative longevity and functional durability are key considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1156
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under the broader group of other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother and is recognized as an obstetric abnormality of the cervix. This condition encompasses three specific subtypes: Cervical shortening, second trimester; Cervical shortening, third trimester; and Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these subtypes is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that a shortened cervical length at any stage of pregnancy can herald the onset of labor before term. The unspecified‑trimester subtype is also directly mapped to the concept of short cervical length in pregnancy, reinforcing its clinical definition. In addition to cervical shortening, Stenosis of cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is another obstetric cervical abnormality, illustrating that multiple cervical pathologies can arise during gestation and the peripartum period. Together, these entities highlight the importance of assessing cervical length and integrity throughout pregnancy, as alterations such as shortening or stenosis are linked to the risk of premature or threatened labor and require careful management to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

It is subdivided into trimester‑specific forms—**Cervical shortening, second trimester**, **Cervical shortening, third trimester**, and **Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester**—each of which is mapped to **Early or threatened labor**, indicating that a shortened cervical length in any trimester serves as a clinical marker for premature or threatened delivery. The unspecified‑trimester form is additionally linked to the concept **Short cervical length in pregnancy**, reinforcing its role as a measurable obstetric parameter. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: Complications mainly related to pregnancy encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric conditions that affect the mother during the maternal pregnancy period, childbirth, and the puerperium. These complications are organized under parent categories such as **Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**, **Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium**, **Early or threatened labor**, **Hemorrhage during pregnancy** (including **abruptio placenta** and **placenta previa**), and **Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**. Cervical pathology is a prominent component of this hierarchy. **Cervical shortening in pregnancy**—including the trimester‑specific forms **cervical shortening, second trimester**, **cervical shortening, third trimester**, and **cervical shortening, unspecified trimester** (synonymous with **short cervical length in pregnancy**)—is classified as an **abnormality of the cervix

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base organizes pregnancy‑related maternal conditions under the umbrella term **Complications mainly related to pregnancy**, which is subdivided into the categories **Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium** and **Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium**. Specific parent concepts within this hierarchy include **Early or threatened labor**, **Hemorrhage during pregnancy** (e.g., abruptio placenta, placenta previa), **Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**, and the broader “other conditions” group. **Cervical shortening in pregnancy** is classified as a child of both “Other conditions or status of the mother…” and “other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” and it is linked via an inverse‑isa relationship to **abnormalities of cervix (obstetric)**. Within the broader context of natural pregnancy cases and outcomes (حالات الحمل الطبيعي ونتائجه (SMQ)), cervical dilation and miscarriage (الكَلَف) represent normal and adverse events. The integrated concepts emphasize how uterine function, pelvic anatomy, fetal presentation, and genital‑tract disorders intersect to influence infertility assessment, labor progression, and the management of high‑risk obstetric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2064
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is classified as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that affect the management of the mother. Within this broader category, cervical shortening, third trimester is identified as a specific manifestation of cervical shortening in pregnancy. Recognizing cervical shortening in pregnancy, especially when it occurs in the third trimester, is important for obstetric care because it signals potential adverse outcomes and guides clinical decisions during both the antenatal and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1049
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother and comprises three specific sub‑entities: cervical shortening, second trimester; cervical shortening, third trimester; and cervical shortening, unspecified trimester. Each of these sub‑entities is classified as early or threatened labor, which is also identified in Arabic as مخاض كاذب (false labor). The unspecified‑trimester form of cervical shortening is additionally mapped to the clinical concept of short cervical length in pregnancy. Together, these relationships illustrate that cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is regarded as a manifestation of early or threatened labor and is considered part of the broader spectrum of maternal complications that influence birth and postpartum care.
